section.title: Divisions in the Church
section.sectionRationale: Having grounded the church in grace, Paul turns at once to the first and root problem: divisions. The Corinthians have fractured into parties rallying around human names, I follow Paul, Apollos, Cephas, or even Christ used as a slogan. Paul's three sharp questions expose the folly: Christ is not divided, Paul was not crucified for them, they were not baptized into Paul's name. The section ends by naming the true center that the party spirit obscures, the cross of Christ, which must not be emptied of its power by clever words.
section.thesis: Party loyalty to human leaders splinters the church and dishonors the one to whom it belongs; the only ground of the church's unity is the crucified Christ, into whose name alone believers are baptized, so eloquence and personality must never eclipse the cross.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the appeal for unity against the party slogans; the three questions that expose the folly of dividing over leaders; and the priority of the cross over eloquence, lest its power be emptied.

units.label: The appeal against parties
units.note: no divisions, but united in mind

verses.text: I appeal to you, brothers, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, that all of you agree together, so that there may be no {{schisma|divisions}} among you and that you may be united in mind and conviction.
My brothers, some from Chloe’s household have informed me that there are {{eris|quarrels}} among you.
What I mean is this: Individuals among you are saying, “I follow Paul,” “I follow Apollos,” “I follow Cephas,” or “I follow Christ.”

units.label: Three questions that expose the folly

verses.text: Is Christ divided? Was Paul crucified for you? Were you {{baptizo|baptized}} into the name of Paul?
I thank God that I did not baptize any of you except Crispus and Gaius,
so no one can say that you were baptized into my name.
Yes, I also baptized the household of Stephanas; beyond that I do not remember if I baptized anyone else.

units.label: The cross above eloquence
units.note: lest the cross be emptied of its power

verses.text: For Christ did not send me to baptize, but to {{euangelizo|preach the gospel}}, not with words of wisdom, lest the {{stauros|cross}} of Christ be emptied of its power.

schisma.gloss: A tear, a split, a division, the word used for a rip in a garment. Applied to the church it pictures the body of Christ being torn along seams of human loyalty. Paul appeals that there be no such rifts, but that the church be knit together in the same mind and judgment.

eris.gloss: Strife, quarreling, contention, rivalry. Reported by Chloe's household, it is the visible symptom of the deeper schism. Paul lists eris elsewhere among the works of the flesh, marking the Corinthian quarrels not as harmless preference but as spiritually serious.

baptizo.gloss: To baptize, to immerse, to identify a person publicly with the one into whose name they are baptized. Paul's point is that no one was baptized into his name; baptism unites believers to Christ, not to a favorite teacher, which is why forming parties around leaders contradicts the very meaning of the rite.

euangelizo.gloss: To announce good news, to preach the gospel. Paul defines his commission by this word: Christ sent him not chiefly to baptize but to proclaim the good news of the cross. The content and power lie in the message itself, not in the eloquence of the messenger.

stauros.gloss: The cross, the Roman instrument of execution, and by extension the saving death of Christ upon it. The cross is the true center that the party spirit obscures. Paul refuses fine rhetoric precisely so that the cross is not emptied of its power, for its power lies in what Christ did, not in how skillfully it is described.

basic.context: Paul moves straight to the church's first great fault: they are divided. People are lining up behind favorite leaders, saying I follow Paul, or Apollos, or Cephas, or even using the name of Christ as a party slogan. Paul pleads for unity and asks three pointed questions to show how foolish this is. Christ is not divided; Paul was not crucified for them; they were not baptized into Paul's name. His calling is to preach the cross, not to build a personal following.
basic.hermeneutics: This is direct pastoral correction. Paul names the problem, its source (report from Chloe's household), and its shape (rival parties), then argues from the meaning of the cross and baptism. The abiding principle is that the church belongs to Christ crucified, so loyalty to human leaders must never fracture it.
basic.originalLanguages: There must be no schisma, no tear in the body. The quarrels are eris, strife. No one was baptizo, baptized, into Paul's name, only into Christ's. Paul's task is to euangelizo, preach the gospel. And all of it centers on the stauros, the cross, which fine words must not empty of its power.
basic.typology: Baptism into the name of Christ fulfills and surpasses the washings and initiations of the old covenant, marking believers as belonging wholly to the Lord. The one crucified for the people answers the sacrificial pattern in which one dies on behalf of many, now fulfilled at the cross.
basic.christ: Christ is the crucified Savior and the sole owner of the church. Paul's three questions all drive to Him: it is Christ who is not to be divided, Christ who was crucified for them, Christ into whose name they were baptized. The cross of Christ is the message Paul is sent to preach, and its power must not be diluted.
basic.god: It is God whom Paul thanks that he baptized so few, so that no personal following could form. God's design is a church united in Christ, not scattered among human champions. The power at work is God's, carried in the plain message of the cross rather than in human eloquence.
basic.crossReferences: Later in the letter Paul returns to Paul and Apollos as mere servants through whom they believed. Ephesians pleads for one body and one Lord. John records Jesus praying that His people would be one. Galatians warns against a gospel dressed up to please men.
basic.oneStory: God is gathering one people around one crucified Lord. The Corinthian parties tear at that unity, but the cross that saves them is also the center that unites them. The whole story runs toward one redeemed people belonging to Christ, not a crowd fragmented behind human names.

context.literary: Verse 10 states the thesis of the whole first section of the letter: no divisions, but unity of mind. Verses 11 and 12 give the report and the slogans; verse 13 fires three rhetorical questions expecting the answer no; verses 14 to 16 give the baptism aside; and verse 17 pivots to the cross and eloquence, launching the great argument of 1:18 to 2:16.
context.historical: Corinth prized rhetoric and celebrity teachers; students commonly attached themselves to a famous orator. The church had imported this culture, ranking Paul, Apollos, and Cephas as rival sophists. Chloe was evidently a believer whose household members carried news to Paul, likely in Ephesus.
context.canonical: The passage stands with the New Testament's repeated calls to unity in Christ and its warnings against dividing the body over personalities. It sets up Paul's sustained contrast between worldly wisdom and the wisdom of the cross that fills the rest of chapters 1 and 2.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To end the party spirit by exposing its folly and refocusing the church on the crucified Christ, and to insist that Paul's ministry, and all Christian ministry, points away from the messenger to the cross, whose power does not depend on human eloquence.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The report of quarrels is description; the appeal for unity and the priority of the cross over eloquence are prescriptive and enduring. Paul's own practice of baptizing few is not a rule against baptism but an illustration that his calling centered on preaching the cross.

debates.question: What is wrong with the group that says I follow Christ?

views.label: A rival party using Christ as a slogan
views.case: Listed alongside the other factions, this group had turned even the name of Christ into a badge of superiority over other believers, making Christ the mascot of one clique rather than the Lord of all.

views.label: Paul's own corrective answer misquoted as a faction
views.case: Some read it as the right rallying cry that the Corinthians had wrongly reduced to just another party line, since belonging to Christ is exactly what verse 13 defends.

debates.bearing: Either way, the point stands: Christ cannot be claimed by a faction to divide the church, for the whole church belongs to the one undivided Christ.

debates.question: Does verse 17 downplay baptism?

views.label: A matter of emphasis, not value
views.case: Paul affirms baptism throughout his letters; here he simply stresses that his primary commission was to preach the gospel, and that the Corinthians' fixation on who baptized them was fueling their factions.

views.label: A caution against tying baptism to a leader's prestige
views.case: By thanking God he baptized so few, Paul removes any basis for a personality cult, showing that the rite unites people to Christ, not to the one who performed it.

debates.bearing: The verse elevates the cross without diminishing baptism; it corrects a distortion that made baptism a party marker rather than union with Christ.

study.originalLanguages: Paul pleads against schisma, a tear in the fabric of the church, and against the eris, strife, reported by Chloe's people. His three questions turn on baptizo: no one was baptized into Paul's name, for baptism unites to Christ alone. He redefines his calling with euangelizo, to preach the gospel, over against merely baptizing. And the whole argument rests on the stauros, the cross of Christ, which words of human wisdom must not empty of its power, since its power lies in the deed of Christ, not the skill of the speaker.

typology.type: Baptism into the name of Christ
typology.fulfillment: Fulfills and surpasses the old covenant washings and initiations, marking the believer as belonging wholly to the Lord rather than to any human leader.
Paul's question, Was Paul crucified for you, points to Christ alone as the one who died on behalf of the people, fulfilling the sacrificial pattern of substitution.

study.christ: Christ crucified is the heart of the passage and the antidote to division. Paul's three questions all converge on Him: Christ is not to be divided into party pieces; Christ, not Paul, was crucified for them; and it was into the name of Christ, not any leader, that they were baptized. Paul's whole commission is to preach the cross of Christ, and he guards that message from being emptied by clever speech, because its saving power resides in what Christ accomplished there.
study.god: God stands behind Paul's ministry and the church's unity. Paul thanks God that he baptized so few, so that no faction could form around him, revealing God's concern that the church cling to Christ and not to men. The power that saves is God's power, carried in the plain word of the cross, so that human eloquence adds nothing and can only obscure it.

crossReferences.note: Paul returns to the Paul and Apollos parties, calling the leaders mere servants through whom God gave the growth.
A plea to keep the unity of the Spirit, one body, one Lord, one faith, one baptism, against all division.
Jesus prays that His people may be one, so the world may believe, the unity the party spirit fractures.
Paul refuses to please men or dress up the gospel, matching his refusal of words of wisdom that would empty the cross.

study.oneStory: God is forming one people around one crucified Lord. The Corinthian factions tear at that unity by exalting human teachers, but the very cross that saves them is the center meant to hold them together. Baptism binds them to Christ, not to a leader, and the message of the cross is the power of God. The story moves toward a single redeemed people who belong to Christ and to Christ alone.

quiz.q: What was the root problem Paul confronts in this passage?

options.text: The church had split into parties rallying around human leaders.
options.note: They were saying I follow Paul, Apollos, Cephas, or Christ, tearing the church into factions.

options.text: The church had stopped baptizing new believers.
options.note: Baptism comes up, but the real issue is division over leaders, not a lack of baptism.

options.text: The church had abandoned the gospel entirely.
options.note: They still held the gospel, but were fracturing over personalities around it.

options.text: The church was persecuting Paul personally.
options.note: The problem is internal division among the Corinthians, not persecution of Paul.

quiz.q: What is the point of Paul's three questions in verse 13?

options.text: To show that Christ alone was crucified for them and that they were baptized into His name, not a leader's.
options.note: The questions expect the answer no and drive all loyalty back to Christ.

options.text: To prove that Paul was the most important of the teachers.
options.note: The opposite; Paul deliberately removes himself as an object of loyalty.

options.text: To suggest that baptism does not matter at all.
options.note: Baptism matters; his point is that it unites to Christ, not to the baptizer.

options.text: To encourage each group to defend its own favorite leader.
options.note: He is dismantling the parties, not endorsing them.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say he preaches not with words of wisdom?

options.text: So that the cross of Christ is not emptied of its power.
options.note: Verse 17 grounds the point: the cross's power must not be obscured by eloquence.

options.text: Because he was incapable of speaking eloquently.
options.note: It is a deliberate choice to protect the message, not an inability.

options.text: Because words and preaching are unimportant.
options.note: He is sent to preach; the point is the content and power of the cross, not silence.

options.text: Because the gospel is too simple to require any explanation.
options.note: The issue is misplaced trust in rhetoric, not the simplicity of the message.

quiz.q: Why does Paul thank God that he baptized so few of the Corinthians?

options.text: So that no one could form a party claiming to be baptized into his name.
options.note: Verses 14 to 15 remove any basis for a personality cult around Paul.

options.text: Because baptism is unnecessary for believers.
options.note: Paul values baptism; he is guarding against factions, not dismissing the rite.

options.text: Because he preferred others to do the harder work.
options.note: His reason is theological, protecting unity, not a matter of preference or labor.

options.text: Because those he baptized had fallen away.
options.note: Nothing suggests that; the point is preventing division around his name.

questions: The Corinthians rallied around favorite teachers. Where do you see yourself, or your church, prizing a personality or tribe in a way that obscures Christ?
Paul asks, Was Christ divided? How should the fact that the whole church belongs to one crucified Lord shape the way you treat believers who differ from you?
Baptism unites you to Christ, not to the one who led you to Him. How does that reframe any pride you might take in your spiritual heritage or mentors?
Paul refused to lean on eloquence so the cross would keep its power. Where might you be tempted to trust presentation or cleverness more than the plain message of the gospel?
Chloe's household cared enough to speak honestly about the quarrels. How can you raise concerns about division in a way that seeks unity rather than adding to the strife?

translationNotes.note: Cephas is the Aramaic name for Peter, meaning rock; Paul uses it to name the same apostle the Gospels call Peter, showing the Corinthian factions even claimed the leading apostles.
The three questions are framed in Greek to expect the answer no; the BSB preserves their force as a rapid triple challenge that undercuts every party.
Words of wisdom translates a phrase for skilled rhetoric or clever eloquence prized in Corinth; the concern is not learning itself but trusting persuasive style in place of the cross's own power.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 1:10-17. Any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas per the no-dash style rule; wording is otherwise unchanged.
